results.title: Raccoon AI: World's First General Purpose Collaborative AI Agent
results.content:
inline, and then you realize you also need a brand identity so you ask for that too. Same session, everything in one workspace, real files you can download or publish. The modalities are practically unlimited. It connects to Gmail, GitHub, Google Drive, Notion, Outlook, and 40+ other tools. You can add your own via custom MCP servers. Raccoon AI is powered by our in-house built agents SDK, ACE, which is currently SOTA on GAIA benchmark with a score of 92.67, we are releasing the technical report on Hacker News today. It's free to start and you can use code PH5X to get one month of Plus 5x for free. Signup here: . I'll be around all day, happy to answer anything and gather a lot of feedback. Find us on X: [...] Everything runs in a sandboxed environment, each session gets it's own isolated computer. The agent's terminal, browser, and file system are containerized. It can't touch anything outside that sandbox unless you've connected a specific integration.
 Custom MCP servers are user-controlled. You bring your own server, you define what tools it exposes, and you control the endpoint. We don't inject anything into that connection.
 On audit/visibility: Every action the agent takes is visible in real-time in the session. You can see its thinking, the commands it runs, the files it creates, and the API calls it makes. Session history is preserved so you can rewind and inspect any step. Raccoon AI today is arguably the most transparent AI agent you will find on the internet. [...] Subscribe

results.title: MuleRun: Raise an AI that actually learns how you work | Product Hunt
results.content:
Here's how it handles complex, multi-tool workflows: every MuleRun user gets a dedicated cloud virtual machine with its own persistent file system, pre-installable native software, and configurable environment. This isn't a sandboxed chat interface — it's a real compute environment where your agent operates continuously. That means it can run terminal commands, manage files, execute scripts, and interact with your tool stack as a native process, not through fragile API wrappers. [...] On the pattern-learning side, MuleRun tracks not just what you ask for, but how you work — the sequence of operations, the tools you reach for in specific contexts, the outputs you accept versus revise. Over time, it builds a working model of your decision logic, so it can begin anticipating the next step in a workflow rather than waiting for instruction.

For a developer workflow spanning terminal sessions and coding environments, the practical implication is that your agent can observe recurring patterns — say, a sequence of build, test, and deploy commands you run in a particular order — and start preparing or executing those proactively. The 24/7 runtime also means long-running processes don't get interrupted when you step away. [...] Report

Ines Defirenza

MuleRun

MuleRun

Maker

@jpeggdev This is a genuinely exciting use case and the architecture maps onto it better than you might expect. MuleRun's 24/7 dedicated VM means you can run sustained, multi-pass optimization loops without babysitting them — set up the evaluation criteria, define what "better" looks like, and let it iterate across 100 passes while you're away. The persistent environment means intermediate results, candidate states, and benchmarks all survive across sessions, so nothing gets lost between runs. For 3D rendering specifically — culling strategies, shading path variants, draw call reduction — the agent can execute, benchmark, log results, and feed winning candidates back into the next generation without manual intervention at each step.

results.title: SureThing.io: Autonomous agent that communicates results like a human | Product Hunt
results.content:
Report

Angelo Bram

Love the chairman, not coder framing. How much business context can SureThing actually absorb before it starts making decisions?

Report

Celine Yu

SureThing.io

SureThing.io

Maker

@angelo\_bram Honest answer: there's no fixed context bar. SureThing doesn't load your business upfront, it absorbs it through the work.

Mechanics: human-in-the-loop on every decision early. You approve, override, or redirect. Each becomes procedural state, not just notes. After enough cycles, the agent makes the call you would've made, and you stop reviewing the boring 80%.

Report

Alice Han

Maker

@angelo\_bram No limit. Try to connect apps which store or manage your business/data, SureThing can absorb connect contexts from them significantly!

Report

123 [...] the chairman. It executes. What makes us different from OpenClaw / Claude Code: They built a terminal. We built a reporting line. AI has no speed limit. Human do. SureThing gives your agents a dashboard to report up — so you stay in control without being the bottleneck. [...] Report

Saul Fleischman

RiteKit Company Logo API

RiteKit Company Logo API

The gap between starred repos and actually running them is real. The execution friction you're solving for is exactly what keeps most teams from shipping with these tools. Curious how you're handling the business context piece — that's usually where things get messy, especially when agents need to make decisions that actually matter to the business.

Report

Celine Yu

SureThing.io

SureThing.io

Maker

@osakasaul memory mechanism is actually our core differentiator. Most agent memory today is note-style. Extract, save, recall. Useful, but you still start from zero every session with better source material.

Ours is closer to procedural memory. We save the state of the work, not just what happened.

results.content:
Haven’t personally used Claude yet, so can’t compare, but if Cursor is helping you learn and ship, that’s already a big win in itself. Would love to hear how it stacks up if you end up testing both!

### Top forum threads [...] The limits are reasonable. For the $100 per month, I use Claude pretty much all the time (often as an unbound agent) and I hardly hit the limits. They have the idea of a session (a five hour window) where you can send something like 250 messages.

It’s really all about how you use the tool. I’ve set up a pretty in-depth system prompt (a local CLAUDE.md file) that describes design, testing, and coding patterns, rules to follow, etc.) it also manages its own plan.md file as it builds so I can tweak a plan on the fly.
